question: What does a typical mobile CI pipeline look like?
answer:
Standard mobile CI stages:

• Lint / static analysis — Detekt + ktlint (Android), SwiftLint + SwiftFormat (iOS), Dart analyze (Flutter).
• Unit tests — fast, JVM/Swift simulator-only, run on every PR.
• UI / instrumented tests — slower, run on emulator (Android) or simulator (iOS); often gated to nightly or main-branch builds.
• Build — debug + release variants. Sign release builds.
• Artifact upload — TestFlight / Play Internal Testing for QA.
• Beta / staged rollout — fastlane lanes or store APIs.
• Symbol upload — Crashlytics / Sentry / Bugsnag for de-obfuscated stack traces.

Providers: GitHub Actions (most common today), Bitrise (mobile-focused), CircleCI, Codemagic (Flutter), Xcode Cloud (iOS-only), Bitbucket Pipelines, Buildkite.

Key rules: PR build < 15 min ideally; cache Gradle / SwiftPM / CocoaPods; never commit signing material — use environment / encrypted secrets.
code_example:
# .github/workflows/android.yml — minimal Android CI
name: Android CI
on:
  pull_request:
    branches: [main]
  push:
    branches: [main]
jobs:
  test:
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
    steps:
      - uses: actions/checkout@v4
      - uses: actions/setup-java@v4
        with: { distribution: 'zulu', java-version: '17' }
      - uses: gradle/actions/setup-gradle@v3
      - run: ./gradlew lint detekt
      - run: ./gradlew testDebugUnitTest
      - uses: actions/upload-artifact@v4
        if: failure()
        with:
          name: test-reports
          path: '**/build/reports/tests/'
  ui-tests:
    runs-on: macos-latest        # for HAXM-accelerated Android emulator
    needs: test
    steps:
      - uses: actions/checkout@v4
      - uses: reactivecircus/android-emulator-runner@v2
        with:
          api-level: 34
          script: ./gradlew connectedDebugAndroidTest

# .github/workflows/ios.yml — minimal iOS CI
# jobs:
#   test:
#     runs-on: macos-14
#     steps:
#       - uses: actions/checkout@v4
#       - run: xcodebuild test -scheme MyApp -sdk iphonesimulator -destination 'platform=iOS Simulator,name=iPhone 15'
#       - run: bundle exec fastlane scan

question: What is Fastlane and what does it automate?
answer:
Fastlane is a Ruby-based automation toolkit for both platforms. You declare 'lanes' (named workflows) in `Fastfile`. Common automations:

• Build & sign — `gym` (iOS), `gradle` (Android).
• Code signing — `match` (iOS) syncs certificates / profiles via a private git repo encrypted at rest.
• Beta distribution — `pilot` (TestFlight), `firebase_app_distribution`, `supply` (Play Store).
• Production — `deliver` (App Store), `supply` (Play Store).
• Screenshots — `snapshot` runs UI tests in multiple locales/devices and uploads.
• Crash reporting — upload dSYMs / mapping files (`upload_symbols_to_crashlytics`).
• Slack / git notifications — `slack`, `git_tag`.

Replaces hand-rolled bash scripts with a tested, cross-platform DSL. Lives next to the project; the same lane runs locally and in CI, reducing 'works on my machine'.
code_example:
# fastlane/Fastfile (Android excerpt)
default_platform(:android)

platform :android do
  desc "Build + upload to Play Internal Testing"
  lane :internal do
    gradle(task: "clean bundleRelease")
    upload_to_play_store(
      track: "internal",
      release_status: "draft",
      aab: "app/build/outputs/bundle/release/app-release.aab",
      mapping_paths: ["app/build/outputs/mapping/release/mapping.txt"]
    )
    slack(message: "Internal build #{lane_context[SharedValues::VERSION_NUMBER]} uploaded.")
  end
end

# fastlane/Fastfile (iOS excerpt)
platform :ios do
  desc "Build + upload to TestFlight"
  lane :beta do
    setup_ci if is_ci
    match(type: "appstore", readonly: is_ci)
    increment_build_number(xcodeproj: "MyApp.xcodeproj")
    build_app(scheme: "MyApp", export_method: "app-store")
    upload_to_testflight(skip_waiting_for_build_processing: true)
    upload_symbols_to_crashlytics(gsp_path: "./MyApp/GoogleService-Info.plist")
  end
end

# In CI
# - bundle exec fastlane android internal
# - bundle exec fastlane ios beta

question: How do you sign and ship an iOS app?
answer:
iOS code signing involves:
• Apple Developer account, Team ID.
• Certificates — Apple Development (debug) + Apple Distribution (release).
• Provisioning profiles — bind an App ID + certificates + devices (for ad hoc).
• Bundle ID — must match the app record in App Store Connect.

Manual signing is fragile ("profile expired", "new device added"). Standard practice:
• `fastlane match` — keep certificates + profiles in a private git repo, encrypted with a passphrase. CI clones, decrypts, installs.
• Or Xcode Cloud / Xcode automatic signing for small teams.

Distribution paths:
• TestFlight (internal up to 100 testers; external up to 10 000 with App Review).
• Ad hoc / Enterprise — limited, mostly legacy.
• App Store — release after review (`fastlane deliver` or App Store Connect).

Gotchas: provisioning profile push capability resets when re-generated; entitlements (push, in-app purchase) must match between profile and code; Xcode Cloud free tier is limited for full pipelines.
code_example:
# fastlane match — keep signing material in a private repo
# 1. fastlane match init
# 2. configure Matchfile:
# git_url("git@github.com:org/certs.git")
# storage_mode("git")
# type("appstore")
# app_identifier(["com.example.app"])
# username("developer@example.com")
#
# 3. fastlane match appstore         # generates / fetches
# 4. fastlane match nuke             # nukes everything (use carefully)

# CI step
# - bundle exec fastlane match appstore --readonly
#   (CI never writes back; provisioned material is read-only)

# Xcode build with explicit signing
# xcodebuild -scheme MyApp \
#   -archivePath MyApp.xcarchive archive \
#   CODE_SIGN_STYLE=Manual \
#   PROVISIONING_PROFILE_SPECIFIER="match AppStore com.example.app" \
#   CODE_SIGN_IDENTITY="Apple Distribution"
#
# xcodebuild -exportArchive \
#   -archivePath MyApp.xcarchive \
#   -exportPath ./build \
#   -exportOptionsPlist ExportOptions.plist

# ExportOptions.plist (snippet)
# <key>method</key><string>app-store</string>
# <key>signingStyle</key><string>manual</string>
# <key>uploadSymbols</key><true/>

# Submission
# bundle exec fastlane pilot upload --ipa ./build/MyApp.ipa
# bundle exec fastlane deliver --ipa ./build/MyApp.ipa

question: How do you sign and ship an Android app?
answer:
Android signing is simpler than iOS but has its own gotchas:

• Upload key — used to sign the AAB you upload to Play Console.
• App signing key — Play App Signing holds it for you (recommended). Google re-signs the artifact for distribution.
• Build types: `debug` (debug.keystore), `release` (your release keystore).
• AAB (Android App Bundle) — preferred over APK since 2021. Play generates per-device APKs (split by ABI, density, language).

Distribution tracks (Play Console):
• Internal testing — up to 100 testers.
• Closed / Open testing — wider audience, manual reviews still required.
• Production with staged rollout (`5% → 20% → 50% → 100%`).

Keystore handling: NEVER commit to git. Store base64 in CI secrets, decode at build time. Lose the upload key → rotate via Play Console (only possible if you used Play App Signing).

Mandatory: target the latest API level (Play policy enforces yearly bumps), declare data safety + privacy policy, ship 64-bit native libs.

question: What are build flavors / variants and when do you use them?
answer:
Flavors let you produce multiple APKs/AABs from one codebase, differing in package name, branding, endpoints, or features.

Android (Gradle):
• `productFlavors { free { ... }; paid { ... } }`
• Combined with `buildTypes { debug; release }` to give variants like `freeDebug`, `paidRelease`.
• Each flavor can override resources, manifests, source sets.

iOS (Xcode):
• Multiple targets — separate builds, separate Info.plist.
• Or schemes + xcconfig files — single target, different config files for dev / staging / prod.
• Asset catalogues + Info.plist substitution for branding.

Flutter:
• `--flavor` flag with `--dart-define` for compile-time constants.

Use flavors for:
• Dev / staging / prod with different API endpoints.
• White-label apps (same codebase, multiple branded apps).
• Free vs paid SKUs.
• Feature subsets per geography.

Keep flavor count small; each one multiplies your CI build matrix.

question: How do you do a staged rollout and observe a release?
answer:
Staged rollout = release to a small percent first, watch metrics, ramp.

Android (Play Console):
• Production → Releases → set rollout percentage (1%, 5%, 20%, 50%, 100%).
• Halt rollout if vitals drop. Resume when fixed (the affected version stays at the lower %).
• Internal app sharing — quick QA URLs.

iOS (App Store):
• Phased release for automatic updates: 1%, 2%, 5%, 10%, 20%, 50%, 100% over 7 days.
• Manual users get the app immediately.
• Pause / resume at any stage.
• Expedited review for critical fixes (use sparingly).

Observability during rollout:
• Crash-free users — Crashlytics / Sentry.
• ANR rate (Android) — Play Vitals.
• Frame rate / startup — Firebase Performance / MetricKit.
• Funnel + business metrics — Mixpanel / Amplitude.
• Server logs — error rate, latency.

Playbook: define KPIs and thresholds BEFORE shipping. Wire alerts (PagerDuty / Slack). Have a rollback plan: feature flags first, app pull-from-store as last resort.
code_example:
# fastlane — staged Play rollout
lane :production_5pct do
  upload_to_play_store(
    track: "production",
    rollout: "0.05",                 # 5%
    aab: "app/build/outputs/bundle/release/app-release.aab"
  )
end

lane :production_50pct do
  upload_to_play_store(
    track: "production",
    rollout: "0.5",
    skip_upload_apk: true,            # already uploaded
    skip_upload_aab: true
  )
end

# Halting via Play Console UI is recommended — fastlane can change rollout but the UI is faster in incidents.

# iOS phased release — toggle in App Store Connect:
# 'Phased Release for Automatic Updates' = ON

# Observability dashboard — track these on a per-version basis:
# - crash-free users (target > 99.5%)
# - ANR rate (target < 0.5%)
# - p99 cold startup (target < 2s)
# - business KPI: signup conversion, checkout success rate

# Rollback decision tree
# 1. Crash spike from new code path -> flip feature flag OFF
# 2. Cannot mitigate via flag -> halt rollout, ship hotfix in next update
# 3. Severe regression -> Play 'halt rollout' / iOS 'pause phased release', expedited review
